title: Solve and Graph Inequalities
why: To solve and graph an inequality, you test numbers to see which make it true, then place them on a number line — that runs on substituting to check and number line basics. Warm up both and this lesson clicks.

diagnostic.q: Which number is a solution of n < 4?
Numbers get bigger as you move which way on a number line?

learn.heading: Testing solutions
learn.intro: A number is a solution of an inequality if it makes the inequality true when you substitute it in. Many numbers can work. Then you show all of them on a number line.
examples: Is 6 a solution of n > 4? Substitute: 6 > 4 is true ✓, so yes.
Is 4 a solution of n > 4? Substitute: 4 > 4 is false ✗, so no — 4 is not greater than itself.

tiers.intro: Substitute and decide true or false.

items.hint: 7 is bigger than 2, so 7 > 2 is true.
3 is bigger than 1, so 3 < 1 is false.

items.q: C. Put x = 4 into x < 6. Is it true?
items.hint: 4 is smaller than 6, so 4 < 6 is true.

items.q: A. Which number is a solution of n > 5?
items.hint: 8 > 5 is true; 5 is not greater than itself, and 2 is too small.

items.q: B. Which number is a solution of n < 3?
items.hint: 1 < 3 is true; 3 is not less than itself, and 5 is too big.

items.q: A. Is 2 a solution of n > 2?
items.hint: n > 2 needs a number bigger than 2; 2 equals 2, so it does not count.

items.q: B. "At least 5" means a number that is…
items.hint: "At least 5" includes 5 and every number above it.

exit.q: 1. Which number is a solution of n > 6?
exit.hint: 9 > 6 is true; 6 is not greater than itself.
x < 3 needs a number smaller than 3; 3 equals 3, so it does not count.
